label: After enduring physical and emotional hardship, the protagonist reaches a solid edge and pulls himself over, symbolizing a breakthrough or achievement.
evidence_text: His arms ached and his breath came in sharp urgent gasps and he climbed through that too, until his hand found an edge—solid, unmoving—and he pulled himself up and over.

label: The climb represents overcoming internal doubts and accepting that some barriers and brokenness are meant to remain, yet the protagonist pushes through.

label: The protagonist climbs a thick, rough rope that is physically painful and emotionally challenging, symbolizing a difficult ascent through barriers.
evidence_text: He stood at the base of a rope—thick, rough-woven hemp, blood-warm and slick with something between sweat and oil, vibrating with the hum of the emptiness it pierced. Each grip burned with tiny splinters. He climbed.

evidence.quote: He stood at the base of a rope—thick, rough-woven hemp, blood-warm and slick with something between sweat and oil, vibrating with the hum of the emptiness it pierced. Each grip burned with tiny splinters. He climbed.

interpretation: The physical description of the rope and the pain in the hands emphasize the somatic cost of the climb.

observation.summary: The protagonist climbs a thick, rough rope that is physically painful and emotionally challenging, symbolizing a difficult ascent through barriers.

evidence.quote: He stood at the base of a rope—thick, rough-woven hemp, blood-warm and slick with something between sweat and oil, vibrating with the hum of the emptiness it pierced. Each grip burned with tiny splinters. He climbed.

label: climbing_as_struggle_vs_climbing_as_triumph

He stood at the base of a rope—thick, rough-woven hemp, blood-warm and slick with something between sweat and oil, vibrating with the hum of the emptiness it pierced.
---
Each grip burned with tiny splinters. He climbed. The air grew thin, then cold, then ringing-silent. A moment of doubt arrived and lodged in him like a stone: some barriers are meant to stand. Some fathers are meant to stay broken.
---
